summary: Gruber eviscerates Brian X. Chen's iPhone 11 review in The New York Times, which advised readers not to upgrade unless their phone is five years old. He argues Chen started with his conclusion and wrote backwards, producing absurd advice like suggesting flash photography instead of upgrading for better low-light capability. Gruber contends the review misrepresents trade-offs between older and newer iPhones, presenting false equivalences that ignore genuine improvements. He frames the review as symptomatic of the Times's broader anti-big-tech editorial stance, comparing it to Soviet propaganda that dismisses genuine advancement out of ideological commitment.
key_points: Chen's advice to keep using a 2015 iPhone ignores that the iPhone 6S is severely outdated in photography, performance, and battery life
Suggesting flash as an alternative to upgrading for low-light photography is backwards — a tech columnist should be telling readers to avoid flash entirely
Chen's comparison of the iPhone X to iPhone 11 presents false trade-offs, ignoring that the new phones have portrait mode too, only better
The review appears to have been written backwards from a predetermined conclusion that nobody should upgrade
The NYT's anti-big-tech editorial posture is producing reviews that read like ideological propaganda rather than useful consumer guidance
money_quotes: This is madness. Just use flash?
The New York Times's institutional crusade against big tech has brought us to this: their personal technology columnist is advising readers to use the flash rather than buy a new phone.

A mainstream technology columnist should be explaining to readers why they should not use flash, and how best to capture low-light photographs without it. 'You could always just use flash' is technology malpractice.
He might as well have written: The iPhone X doesn't have an ultra-wide lens, but, on the other hand, it does have a crappier version of the telephoto lens.
The whole piece reads like Soviet propaganda, explaining why Soviet-built cars are just fine, they're great even.
The Times is treating the tech industry the way Pravda treated the West.
Big tech deserves scrutiny — including Apple. But it's starting to come across as a mindless dogmatic crusade.
I look forward to Chen telling Android users to keep using 2015 phones, too. I see people every day raving about the performance of their Galaxy S6 and Google Nexus 6.
key_insight: When media outlets let anti-tech ideology drive product reviews, they end up producing advice that actively harms readers — like recommending flash photography in 2019.
